text: We began on the left side due to the size of that mass.

We wanted to make sure that it didn't involve any structures and thankfully it was not involving the pericardium or the lung despite there being adhesions which were taken down with ultrasonic shears.
Exposure can definitely be a concern of a minimally invasive approach to a mass size this large, as it can involve the left phrenic nerve.
We found that the left phrenic nerve was quite adherent, but was able to be successfully dissected and preserved.
The mass was eventually fully dissected and placed in a laparoscopic bag.
It was removed and then sent to pathology.
On frozen section, the mass was suspicious for thymoma.

Given this, we directed our attention to the right side and proceeded with a right VATS thymectomy via right-sided ports to ensure that the right phrenic nerve was clearly free and to gain better access to the cervical horns.
